coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent paper-based analytical devices for ultra-sensitive dual-type RNA detections and accurate gastric cancer screening
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yuqian
Li, Ruyi
Liang, Fangyuan
Deng, Chao
Seidi, Farzad
Xiao, Huining
coredata.dc:description: Demand on the quick screening of gastric cancer (GC) has significantly stimulated the development of biomarker sensing techniques. Herein, we report the novel fluorescent paper-based analytical devices (PADs) for detections of GC-related microRNA-21 (miRNA-21) and circular RNA from Hippocampus Abundant Transcript 1 gene (circRNA-HIAT1) with prominent reliability and sensitivity. The PADs, constructed by in-situ synthesis of blue-emissive carbon dots (CDs) and conjugations of probe DNAs, exhibit the superior uniformity and stability. In the presence of targets, rolling circle amplifications (RCA) are triggered to generate long DNA strands for the assemblies of green-/red-emissive labels. Consequently, remarkable blue-to-green and blue-to-red emission color transitions of the PADs are achieved, implementing the color-analysis of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1, respectively. Benefited from the efficient RCA, coupled with the drastic ratiometric fluorescent changes, the limit of detections (LODs) of PADs are found to be several fM with the upper limit of the linear detection range at 1 nM. More importantly, the fluorescent PADs possess excellent specificity, as well as anti-interference capability in biological settings, enabling their applications in accurate GC screening with plasma samples. Overall, the proposed fluorescent PADs are featured with robust sensing platform, facile signal readout, and exceptional dual-type RNA sensing performance, holding high potential in point-of-care testing (POCT).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Paper-based analytical device
Gastric cancer
Carbon dot
Color-analysis
MicroRNA
Circular RNA

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 analysis in clinical samples
Fluorescent PADs constructed by in-situ synthesis of CDs and bioconjugation
Color engineering for dual-type RNA detections
Ultra-sensitive miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 detections based on color-analysis
Accurate GC screening by simultaneous assays of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 in clinical samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Metasequoia Faculty Start-up Research Fund of Nanjing Forestry University

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20210630 ); the Metasequoia Faculty Start-up Research Fund of Nanjing Forestry University; and NSERC Canada. We thank Dr. Qu for his kind provision of clinical plasma samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of fibers homogenized from a) cellulose paper, b) CA modified paper, and c) carbonization treated paper with CA modification; And d) fluorescence spectra of CDs in-situ synthesized on paper and those synthesized in aqueous solution. (B) Fluorescent images and intensities of paper sheets with a) in-situ synthesized CDs and b) physically adsorbed CDs. The sheets were sonicated for varying times in prior to intensity measurements of randomly selected areas (white squares).
(A) Fluorescence spectra of gQDs and rQDs. (B) The CIE chromaticity coordinates of CDs, gQDs, and rQDs, which are calculated from their emission profiles. (C) TEM images of gQD-sDNA1 and rQD-sDNA2.

(A) The agarose gel electrophoresis (top panel) and AFM images (bottom panel) of RCA reaction solutions in the presence/absence of miRNA-21 or circRNA-HIAT1. Marker: 100–5000 bp. (B) Digital photographs, and the split fluorescence channels (B-ch: blue channel; G-ch: green channel; R-ch: red channel) of fluorescent PADs in the presence of increased concentrations (0–2000 pM) of miRNA-21 or circRNA-HIAT1 (top panel). CIE chromaticity diagram showing color-shifts of PAD1 and PAD2 with increasing amounts of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1, respectively (bottom left), and evolutions of the color-shift distances of fluorescent PADs along with the miRNA-21 or circRNA-HIAT1 abundances (bottom right). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Color-shift distances and fluorescent images of PAD1 upon detections of (A) miRNA-21 or (B) 1 BM sequence in their mixtures, and those of PAD2 exposed to varying molar fractions of circRNA-HIAT1 (C) or mRNA (D). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Fluorescent images of (A) PAD1 and (B) PAD2 exposed to serum samples from healthy people, GC, and NSCLC patients. Color-shift distances of PAD1 (triangles) and PAD2 (squares) are plotted in the zoomed CIE coordination system. The box charts show the calculated abundance of miRNA-21 or circRNA-HIAT1, respectively, from three groups of samples. The data are compared by t-test. ***P < 0.001, **P < 0.01, and “ns” means no significance. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Illustration of fluorescent PADs construction and color-analysis of dual-type RNA. (A) CA modification of cellulose paper and in-situ synthesis of CDs. (B) miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 triggered RCA and fluorescent labeling of PADs. (C) Imaging of the fluorescent PADs arrays with smartphone and chromaticity diagram based color-analysis of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent paper-based analytical devices for ultra-sensitive dual-type RNA detections and accurate gastric cancer screening

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Innovation Center for Forest Chemicals and Materials and Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Innovation Center for Forest Chemicals and Materials and Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources
College of Light Industry and Food Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Innovation Center for Forest Chemicals and Materials and Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of New Brunswick, Fredericton, New Brunswick, E3B5A3, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of New Brunswick, Fredericton, New Brunswick, E3B5A3, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Demand on the quick screening of gastric cancer (GC) has significantly stimulated the development of biomarker sensing techniques. Herein, we report the novel fluorescent paper-based analytical devices (PADs) for detections of GC-related microRNA-21 (miRNA-21) and circular RNA from Hippocampus Abundant Transcript 1 gene (circRNA-HIAT1) with prominent reliability and sensitivity. The PADs, constructed by in-situ synthesis of blue-emissive carbon dots (CDs) and conjugations of probe DNAs, exhibit the superior uniformity and stability. In the presence of targets, rolling circle amplifications (RCA) are triggered to generate long DNA strands for the assemblies of green-/red-emissive labels. Consequently, remarkable blue-to-green and blue-to-red emission color transitions of the PADs are achieved, implementing the color-analysis of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1, respectively. Benefited from the efficient RCA, coupled with the drastic ratiometric fluorescent changes, the limit of detections (LODs) of PADs are found to be several fM with the upper limit of the linear detection range at 1 nM. More importantly, the fluorescent PADs possess excellent specificity, as well as anti-interference capability in biological settings, enabling their applications in accurate GC screening with plasma samples. Overall, the proposed fluorescent PADs are featured with robust sensing platform, facile signal readout, and exceptional dual-type RNA sensing performance, holding high potential in point-of-care testing (POCT).

ce:section.ce:para: Gastric cancer (GC) is the third leading cause of cancer-related death worldwide (Machlowska et al., 2020). Despite great progress in the understanding of molecular mechanisms of oncogenesis and diagnosis has been made, the survival rate of GC patients remains unsatisfying (Yasufukul et al., 2020). Aiming at quick screening GC at the early-stage and reducing unnecessary endoscopies, a series of predictive biomarkers, including circulating tumor cells, circulating cell-free DNA, microRNA (miRNA), or exosomes, and their detection methods have been explored in the past decades (Matsuoka and Yashiro, 2018; Daneshpour et al., 2018; Yasui et al., 2004; Kao et al., 2015). MiRNAs are a class of small sized (19–25 nucleotides in length) and non-coding RNAs that play key regulatory roles by blocking mRNA in many biological processes including cell fate determination, proliferation, and cell death (Guo et al., 2017). Aberrant expression of miRNAs is believed to be associated with human diseases, cancer in particular. To date, the discovered miRNAs involved in GC development are steadily increasing and their expression patterns have been provided for GC screening (Sawaki et al., 2018; Ueda et al., 2010). In light of the complicated gene expression regulating network where miRNAs are involved, the high accuracy of GC diagnosis still remains challenging. For instance, over-expressions of miRNA-21, miRNA-191, and miRNA-196a/b and down-regulations of miRNA-101, miRNA-195 and miRNA-383 have been attempted in GC patients. However, for the patients with lung cancer, the miRNA expression pattern exhibits significant overlap with that of GC patients (Uddin and Chakraborty, 2018; Inamura and Ishikawa, 2016). A possible approach to address this problem is to develop sensing platforms that can simultaneously evaluate the expression levels of not only miRNA, but also other types of non-coding RNAs, which possess intrinsic relationships with miRNA in oncogenesis and progression of GC.

Circular RNAs (circRNAs) are special long non-coding RNAs formed by back-splicing of introns during the biogenesis of mRNA (Kristensen et al., 2019; Ashwal-Fluss et al., 2014). Because of their sequence homology, circRNA can competitively sponge miRNAs and eliminate the effects of miRNAs in blocking mRNA, playing the role as a gene expression regulator (Hansen et al., 2013; Meng et al., 2017; Geng et al., 2018). Therefore, circRNAs can also be considered as the markers of cancers and used as clinical strategies to measure disease status. As to the occurrence of GC, it has been reported that expression of circular RNA from Hippocampus Abundant Transcript 1 gene (circRNA-HIAT1) is down-regulated, conversely, obvious up-regulation of miRNA-21 was validated (Quan et al., 2020). CircRNA-HIAT1 is capable of inhibiting cell proliferation and promoting apoptosis through sponging miRNA-21 and suppressing extracellular signal-regulated kinase signaling pathways. As such, subspecies of circRNA-HIAT1 (ID: has_circ_0000096) was developed as a GC indicator with significant diagnostic value (Li et al., 2017). Recently, several papers have documented the circRNA detection methods, overwhelmingly relying on specific recognition of the junction sites of circRNAs over their linear homologues (Panda et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2020a, 2020b; Boss and Arenz, 2020). However, incapability in uncovering the quantitative relationships between circRNA and the corresponding regulatory objectives (miRNAs) significantly limits the cancer diagnosis accuracy.

Paper-based analytical devices (PADs), being featured with cost-effectiveness, operational simplicity, long-term stability, and more importantly, prominent multiplexing capability by signal or spatial encoding (El-Shaheny et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2019), have attracted enormous interests in assays of nucleic acid-based biomarkers (Liu et al., 2019; Suea-Ngam et al., 2021; Scida et al., 2013; Zheng et al., 2020; Teengam et al., 2021). In the PADs, the cellulose paper with large surface area and abundant pores enables the immobilization of capture probes or biosensors for analyzing samples, whereby the target binding is detectable as the color (Li et al., 2019a; Chen et al., 2017), electricity (Narang et al., 2018; Li et al., 2015), fluorescence (Seok et al., 2020), or other analytical signals (Wang et al., 2020; Qi et al., 2017) of the PADs are changed. Among them, fluorescent PADs received widespread acceptance due to its inherent high sensitivity and ease of implementation. More importantly, the sensing signal can be directly visualized by naked-eyes or quantitatively analyzed by smartphone under UV irradiation (Noor and Krull, 2014; Noor et al., 2015; Liang et al., 2017), which are of great significance for the achievement of point-of-care testing (POCT). Generally, working principles of the fluorescent PADs can be categorized as two types: 1) the target is captured by the immobilized oligonucleotides and, subsequently, the binding event is labeled by fluorophore. Examples are found in the lateral flow test strips or microfluidic PADs (Rosa et al., 2014; Dong et al., 2019) with typical “turn on” sensing signal. 2) Mono-dispersed fluorescent biosensors are post-immobilized on paper substrates to fabricate PADs, of which the signals are transduced in “turn off-on” mode or ratiometric fluorescence by the mechanisms of Föster resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Zhou et al., 2014; Noor and Krull, 2013), or bioluminescence resonance energy transfer (BRET) (Li et al., 2019b). Reliability of the later is remarkably promoted since they are capable of eliminating the interferences brought by environment factors. However, the post-immobilization strategy involves chemical coupling or physical adsorption (printing, dipping, etc.) (Liu et al., 2016) between fluorescent biosensors and cellulose fibers, being accompanied by either alteration in the chemical and optical properties of the biosensors or diffusion susceptibility of the biosensors in the application process. Therefore, advanced fabrication strategy is urgently needed for the next generation of fluorescent PADs, which can meet the requirements of ultra-sensitivity, high reliability and field-deployable analysis, and finally realize the goal of POCT.

In this work, we report the fabrication of novel fluorescent PADs and their application in detections of GC-related dual-type RNA: miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 (has_circ_0000096). Aiming at promoting the uniformity and stability of the PADs, as well as the reliability in RNA detection, an unprecedented tactic for fluorescent functionalization of cellulose paper is proposed. As shown in Scheme 1 A, citric acid (CA) is densely modified onto cellulose fibers through esterification, and subsequently in-situ carbonized to form blue-emissive carbon dots (CDs) with high quantum yield (QY), excellent uniformity, and desirable structural stability. Further, by multiplexing the conjugated probe.

DNAs (pDNA1 and pDNA2) and engineering emission colors of fluorescent labels, the PADs are endowed with dual-type RNA responsiveness (Scheme 1B). PAD1 with pDNA1 and PAD2 with pDNA2 are used for assays of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1, respectively. Relying on the base pairing of pDNA1 and the central part of an auxiliary padlock DNA (Table S1), the circulation of the padlock DNA and rolling circle amplification (RCA) can be successively triggered upon the exposure to miRNA-21. Whereas pDNA2 is designed to specifically recognize the back-splicing site of circRNA-HIAT1 and directly extended by RCA, in which the target plays the role as template. Green-emissive quantum dot labeled signal DNA1 (gQD-sDNA1) and red-emissive quantum dot labeled signal DNA2 (rQD-sDNA2) are responsible for the ratiometric fluorescent signal outputs of PAD1 and PAD2, respectively. As the results of the hybridization events between signal DNAs and RCA products, associations of QDs, on one hand, can generate the green or red fluorescence on the PADs, and on the other hand, can quench the blue fluorescence of CDs in the mechanism of FRET. Changes in the relative fluorescence intensity allow ultra-sensitive miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 detections via the drastic color evolutions of the PADs, which are readily subjected to the portable device and chromaticity diagram-based color-analysis (Scheme 1C). Further relying on the pathological relationship of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1, the proposed fluorescent PADs are successfully demonstrated to be capable of accurately screening GC among normal people or other types of cancers.

Citric acid (CA, 99%) was purchased from J&K Scientific. Ethanediamine (EDA, 99.5%), quinine sulfate (99%), rhodamine 6G (99%), rhodamine B (99%), and sodium hypophosphite monohydrate (99%) were obtained from Aladdin. Whatman Grade 42 cotton-based cellulose paper was used as the substrate. All the DNA and RNA sequences were synthesized by GeneScript (Nanjing, China). 1-(3-(Dimethylamino)propyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 99%), Rnase inhibitor (2000 units), deoxynucleotide triphosphates mix (dNTPs, each at 10 mM), M-MLV reverse transcriptase and GenElute™ plasma/serum RNA purification kit were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. T4 DNA ligase and phi29 polymerase were purchased from New England Biolabs Ltd. (Beijing, China). RevertAidTM cDNA synthesis kits were received from Thermo Scientific. Pure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was obtained from a Pall Cascade AN system.

CA was firstly decorated onto cellulose paper via Fisher-Speier esterification following the reported method (de Cuadro et al., 2015). For the in-situ synthesis of CDs, 0.5 g of CA modified cellulose paper was placed in a flask containing 25 mL of water and EDA, and heated to 160 °C in a microwave reactor with a power of 400 W. The fluorescence property of in-situ synthesized CDs was optimized by controlling the amount of EDA and the time of microwave radiation. After reaction, the fluorescent paper was washed with water for several times. Subsequently, naturally dried fluorescent paper was cut into round shaped sheets with a diameter of 6 mm, which was nearly the same as the size of microplate wells (96-well). Conjugations of pDNA1 and pDNA2 onto fluorescent paper were realized via the carbodiimide reaction. Each paper sheet was immersed in one well of the plate containing 100 μL of 2-morpholinoethanesulfonic acid buffer (MES, 100 mM, pH 6.0). Afterwards, 10 μL of mixed solution of pDNA1 or pDNA2 (2 μM) with EDC (50 mg/mL) was added into the well, followed by standing for 4 h. Finally, the fluorescent PAD1 and PAD2 were washed with Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.5, containing 50 mM MgCl2) for 3 times and stored in Tris-HCl buffer for further use.

Dual-colored InP/ZnS QDs were synthesized using a one-pot approach (Xu et al., 2008). Water solubility of the QDs was achieved by replacing the nonpolar ligand with 3-mercaptopropionic acid. The fluorescent labels (gQD-sDNA1 and rQD-sDNA2) were constructed via the metal affinity coordination. Briefly, 500 μL of gQD solution (0.5 mg/mL) was mixed with 10 μL of thiolated sDNA1 solution (100 μM) and the mixture was stirred for 12 h, followed by ultrafiltration for 3 times (3000 rpm, 10 min) with 30 KDa centrifugal filter. The same procedure was performed for the construction of rQD-sDNA2.

The RCA strategy was employed for enhancing the sensitivity of dual-type RNA detections following the protocol reported elsewhere (Hu et al., 2020). Conditions of the RCA were optimized in homogeneous solutions by quantifying the amount of extended DNA strands with 5′-FAM and 3′-BHQ1 labeled molecular beacons (MB1 and MB2). For miRNA-21 triggered RCA, Tris-HCl buffer solutions (120 μL) were spiked with pDNA1 (0.2 μM), padlock DNA (0.5 μM), Rnase inhibitor (100 U), and miRNA-21 (1 nM). After incubation for 1 h at 37 °C, 15 μL of ligation buffer (10 × ) and 15 μL of T4 DNA ligase with varying concentrations were added and incubated at 25 °C for 1 h. Afterwards, 24 μL of 10 × polymerization buffer, 24 μL of phi29 polymerase (0–20 U/μL), different volumes of dNTPs (each at 10 mM), and pure water were added to fill up the solution to 240 μL and incubated at 37 °C for different time. The polymerization was terminated by heating the solution to 65 °C for 10 min. Finally, an excessive amount of MB1 (10 μL, 100 μM) was added and the fluorescence of MB1 was measured after standing for 30 min. For cirRNA-HIAT1 triggered RCA, the optimal conditions were also determined, including the concentrations of M-MLV reverse transcriptase and dNTPs, as well as the polymerization time, via labeling the RCA product with MB2.

The RNA detections were conducted in 96-well microplates with 20 μL of Tris-HCl buffer in each well following the target binding-RCA reaction-labeling procedure. The concentrations of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 ranged from 0 to 2 nM in the quantitative analysis. The concentrations for ligase, phi29 polymerase, reverse transcriptase, and dNTPs, coupled the RCA reaction time were kept consistent in terms of those optimized in homogeneous solution. Termination of the RCA processes were conducted by removing the reaction buffer, and washing the PADs with Tris-HCl buffer for 5 times. For labeling the RCA products, 25 μL of gQD-sDNA1 or rQD-sDNA2 dispersions (0.1 mg/mL) were added into the wells, and a 30 min incubation was performed prior to washing away of unhybridized fluorescent labels. For the specificity test, totally 1 nM of mixtures of miRNA-21 and its one-base mismatched (1 BM) sequence, as well as 1 nM of mixtures of circRNA-HIAT1 and its corresponding linear mRNA were, respectively, added into PAD1 and PAD2, and subjected to RCA and fluorescent labeling. The anti-interference capability of the PADs was tested by detecting miRNA-21 or circRNA-HIAT1 with the coexistence of potential interferents.

The digital fluorescent images of the fluorescent PADs were taken in the darkroom using a smart phone (Sumsang, Galaxy S10, Korea). For illuminating the PADs, a hand-held UV lamp (365 nm) was placed above the PADs at a distance of ca. 30 cm. The chromaticity coordinates (x,y) of the PADs were calculated according to equations (1)–(3):
(1)
[
X
Y
Z
]
=
1 0.17697
[
0.49
0.31
0.20
0.17697
0.81240
0.01063
0.00
0.01
0.99
]
[
R
G
B
]
(2)
x =
X
X + Y + Z
(3)
y =
Y
X + Y + Z
where R, G, and B values were acquired by analyzing the fluorescent images of PADs with ImageJ software. The normalized matrix transform on the obtained R, G, and B values can deduce the XYZ tristimulus values. Finally, the obtained XYZ tristimulus values were deliberately designed to characterize the chromaticity of a color by two chromaticity coordinate values x and y.

ce:section.ce:section-title: miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 analysis in clinical samples
ce:section.ce:para: Plasma samples from 5 cases of GC patients, 5 cases of nonsmall cell lung cancer (NSCLC) patients, as 5 normal persons were used to evaluate the potential POCT applications of the proposed fluorescent PADs. The total RNA was extracted from plasma (200 μL) according to the technical description of GenElute™ purification kit. For miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 detections, the eluted total RNA solutions were respectively added into the microplate wells with fluorescent PAD1 and PAD2. After 1 h of incubation at 37 °C, RCA and fluorescent labeling processes were conducted. The abundances of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 were calculated based on the (x,y) chromaticity coordinate of each PAD and the established calibration curves.

The levels of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 were estimated by quantitative reverse transcription PCR (qRT-PCR) as well. Calibration curves for quantification of targets with qRT-PCR were generated with varying amounts of synthetic miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1, respectively. Both synthetic and extracted RNAs were firstly reversed transcribed with RevertAidTM cDNA synthesis kits. Subsequently, the qRT-PCR amplifications of the cDNAs were conducted in PCRTubes with SYBR Green, dNTP mixture, primers and cDNAs in a 20 μL volume. The Junction-spanning divergent primers specific for circRNA-HIAT1 were designed using free online web tool CircInteractome. (https://circinteractome.irp.nia.nih.gov/circular_rna.html).

X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements of paper substrates were performed on an AXIS UltraDLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired with a JEM-2100 instrument (JEOL, Japan). The cellulose paper, CA modified paper, and fluorescent paper were disintegrated into cellulose fibers, CA modified cellulose (cellulose-CA), and cellulose with in-situ synthesized CDs (cellulose-CDs), respectively, using a dispersion homogenizer (FJ200-SH, China), followed by dropping 10 μL of dispersion solution onto the copper grid and drying naturally. For Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) measurements, the abovementioned fiber dispersions were freeze dried into powder. The spectra were acquired on a Vertex 80V FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, German) using KBr discs containing cellulose, cellulose-CA, and cellulose-CDs, respectively. All fluorescence tests were performed on an FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh instruments, UK) equipped with a SC-05 standard cuvette holder for solutions and a SC-10 solid sample holder for fluorescent paper or PADs. The QY of fluorescent paper was measured using diluted cellulose-CDs fiber dispersion with quinine sulfate as the reference (EX: 340 nm, QY: 54% in 0.05 M sulfuric acid). The QYs of gQDs and rQDs were measured by the use of rhodamine 6G (EX: 488 nm, QY: 94% in ethonal) and rhodamine B (EX: 495 nm, QY: 89% in ethonal) as the references, respectively. The morphological and compositional analyses were conducted on a JSM-7600F scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with X-ray Energy Dispersive Spectroscope (EDS) (Japan). The images of RCA products were acquired from atomic force microscope (Bio-MFP3D, UK). The qRT-PCR experiments were carried out on an ABI 7500 Real-Time PCR System.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent PADs constructed by in-situ synthesis of CDs and bioconjugation
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent PADs were prepared by a successive esterification-carbionization process, followed by conjugations of pDNAs. Under the catalysis of hypophosphites, the carboxyl group of CA reacts with C6 hydroxyl group of cellulose, leading to the highly dense modification of cellulose with CA and obvious weight gain (ca. 14%) of the paper substrate (Fig. S1 in the SI). Taking the advantage of carbonization easiness of CA, blue-emissive CDs are facilely synthesized on the cellulose paper. Fig. 1 A shows the TEM images of cellulose, cellulose-CA, and cellulose-CDs. In comparison, distribution of CDs is conspicuously observed for the composites of cellulose-CDs. The XPS and FTIR results further confirm the successful decoration of CA and formation of CDs on cellulose paper. High-resolution C1s spectra emphasizes the CA modification of cellulose paper by showing the CO bond at 288.7 eV (Fig. S2) (Liu et al., 2021). Moreover, the appearance of N1s signal on the spectrum of cellulose-CDs composites can prove the formation of CDs and nitrogen doping during the carbonization process.

Similar conclusion can be deduced from the FTIR measurement. The stretching vibration of CO (1712 cm−1) introduced by CA suddenly arises on the spectrum of cellulose-CA, however, sharply decreases on the spectrum of cellulose-CDs due to the carbonization of CA (Fig. S3). The successful formation of CDs renders cellulose paper with bright blue fluorescence. Optimally, the paper shows an emission peak at 453 nm and a QY of 43% upon the addition of 100 μL of EDA and after conducting the microwave radiation for 15 min in the carbonization process (Fig. S4). The high similarity in fluorescence profiles, as well as the QYs, for fluorescent paper and CDs synthesized in aqueous solution (EM: 451 nm, QY: 47%) indicates that the paper substrate negligibly impacts on the optical properties of CDs.

More importantly, marvelous uniformity and superior diffusion resistance of CDs are achieved via the in-situ synthesis strategy. As shown in Fig. 1B, under the illumination of a hand-held UV lamp, both as-prepared paper sheets with in-situ synthesized CDs and physically adsorbed CDs show bright blue emission. After twice sonication (10 s for each time), the emission intensity of the sheet with in-situ synthesized CDs remains unchanged, revealing excellent anti-diffusion capacity. The brightness for randomly analyzed areas within the sheet is satisfactorily consistent (RSD ≤2.2%), regardless of the sonication. Contrarily, acute decrement in brightness is observed for cellulose paper with adsorbed CDs as sonication treatment was performed, and the RSD could be larger than 50% since the CDs are unevenly diffusing during the treatment. The results suggest that the proposed in-situ fluorescent functionalization strategy can provide an ultra-stable platform for fluorescent PADs, which are tolerant to repetitious washing steps. Being aware of its advanced characteristics, a novel type of fluorescent PADs responsive to RNA biomarkers was exploited by conjugating pDNA1 or pDNA2 onto the cellulose-CDs substrate. The conjugation was achieved by use of the well-known carbodiimide reaction, and a FAM-labeled pDNA1 was applied for calculating the number of conjugated pDNA (Fig. S5). By recording the decrement in fluorescence intensity of FAM in solution, the conjugation density was quantified as 5.6 × 1011 strands/mm2. The superior conjugation efficiency stems from the porous structure of the paper substrate and abundant reactive sites (uncarbonized carboxyl groups) on cellulose and CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color engineering for dual-type RNA detections
ce:section.ce:para: Color engineering of the fluorescent PADs was performed with purpose of outputting ratiometric fluorescence in the presence of RNA targets. Dual-emissive InP/ZnS QDs with fluorescence peaks at 512 nm and 610 nm (Fig. 2 A), respectively, can emit bright green (QY: 27%) and red light (QY: 21%), and therefore, are selected as the fluorophores. The chromaticity coordinates of gQDs, rQDs, together with that of blue-emissive CDs, constitute a triangle area, which covers the three primary colors on the CIE coordination system (Fig. 2B). As such, the fluorescent PADs are expected to reveal distinct color changes upon the presence of RNA targets. Relying on the high coordination affinity of thiols and metal, thiolated sDNA1 and sDNA2 are capable of anchoring onto the surface of gQDs and rQDs by replacing the dangling ligand, respectively, forming the nanohybrids of gQD-sDNA1 and rQD-sDNA2. The successful constructions of the nanohybrids can be verified by monitoring the electrophoretic mobility of gQDs and rQDs, as well as the nanohybrids in a 3% agarose gel. The typical gel images where samples of gQD-sDNA1and rQD-sDNA2 were run with control samples of gQDs and rQDs show that the nanohybrids exhibit higher migration rates (Fig. S6). The results indicate that additional quantities of negative charges are introduced on surface of QDs due to the anchoring of DNA phosphate. From the TEM images of the nanohybrids (Fig. 2C), the sizes of gQDs and rQDs are as small as 2.6 ± 0.4 nm and 3.1 ± 0.6 nm, respectively, along with excellent mono-dispersity. These merits are favorable to assembling them onto PADs and transducing the target binding events to fluorescence signal with high efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultra-sensitive miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 detections based on color-analysis
ce:section.ce:para: For miRNA-21, the detection follows target binding-padlock DNA ligation-RCA reaction-labeling procedure, whist for detection of circRNA-HIAT1, auxiliary padlock DNA and ligation are not required by taking the advantage of the circular structure of target. The bindings of targets can be verified by their remarkable absorbance decrement at 260 nm after being added to the fluorescent PADs (Fig. S7). However, no significant absorbance changes are observed upon adding the targets onto bare fluorescent paper sheets without probe DNA conjugations, implying the excellent resistance to nonspecific adsorption. Based on its highly efficient signal amplification and point-mutation discrimination capability, target triggered RCA was integrated on fluorescent PADs to confer the assay with ultra-sensitivity and high specificity. The RCA efficiency was firstly evaluated in homogeneous solution via recoding the fluorescence intensity of MB1 and MB2. The optimized concentrations for T4 DNA ligase, phi29 polymerase, dNTPs are 1 U/μL, 1 U/μL, and each at 500 μM, respectively; and 1 h for the polymerization for miRNA-21 triggered RCA. While the optimal conditions for circRNA-HIAT1 triggered RCA are 1 U/μL of reverse transcriptase, 500 μM of each dNTP, and 1 h for reverse transcription (Fig. S8). The RCA reactions can be proved by the agarose gel electrophoresis and AFM experiments. As shown in Fig. 3 A, in the presence of targets, the almost un-migrated electrophoresis bands on agarose gels, coupled with the appearances of RCA chains in the AFM images, suggest the acquisitions of high-molecular weight RCA chains. However, no migration bands in the gels or DNA chains in AFM images were observed due to the too small chain size of probe DNAs in the absence of targets. To further verify the RCA reaction and signal labeling on fluorescent PADs, EDS elemental mapping analysis was conducted step-wisely during the miRNA-21 detection (Fig. S9). The consecutive emergences of nitrogen, phosphorus and zinc indicate the realizations of in-situ formation of (nitrogen doped) CDs, target triggered DNA chain extension, and assembly of InP/ZnS QD labeled sDNA1.

Having validated the feasibility of fluorescent PADs in RNA detections, their fluorescent responsiveness, as well as analytical performances were investigated using known concentrations of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescent PADs display continuous fluorescence color transitions from blue to green/red upon improving the miRNA-21/circRNA-HIAT1 abundance. Using ImageJ software, the split emission intensities of blue and green/red channels of the images were acquired. The gradually increased brightness of green or red fluorescence is ascribed to the elevated assembling number of gQD-sDNA1 or rQD-sDNA2 in the presence of greater amounts of targets. Surprisingly, it is found that the brightness of blue channel for both groups is suppressed especially in the target concentration range of 0–50 pM, which can effectively accelerate the color transition of PADs and thus promote the sensitivity of the detections. Evolutions in the green-to-blue and red-to-blue fluorescence intensity ratio of the PADs (Fig. S10) are in agreement with the tendency shown by their color splitting. One reasonable explanation for the fluorescence quenching of blue-emissive CDs is that the formation of FRET pairs between CDs (donors) and proximal gQDs or rQDs (acceptors), on the basis of apparent spectral overlaps (Fig. S11). To quantify the target abundance dependent color changes, (x,y) chromaticity coordinates of the fluorescent PADs were calculated. The chromaticity coordinates of PAD1 and PAD2 are shifted linearly toward those of gQDs and rQDs with increased amounts of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1, respectively, composing the calibration curves for the quantification of targets. The net color-shift distances (square root of the sum of Δx square and Δy square, Δx and Δy refer to the net shift distances of chromaticity coordinates in x axis and y axis, respectively) of the fluorescent PADs display excellent linearity against the logarithm of targets concentrations ranged from 0.1 pM to 1 nM. For the calibration curves, the regression equations are sqrt (Δx 2 + Δy 2) = 0.074lgC miRNA-21 + 0.089 (R 2 = 996), and sqrt (Δx 2 + Δy 2) = 0.077lgC circRNA-HIAT1 + 0.106 (R 2 = 999). Accordingly, the calculated limits of detections (LODs, 3δ) are 3.5 fM and 2.7 fM for miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1, respectively, which are comparable to those most sensitive platforms for RNA detections (Table S2). The efficient RCA reaction, as well as high signal-to-noise ratio endowed from FRET effect and remarkable QYs of fluorescent labels, are considered to contribute to the ultra-sensitivity of the proposed PADs.

To evaluate the specificity of the fluorescent PADs, 1 nM of (miRNA-21 + 1 BM sequence) with continuously changed molar fractions, as well as 1 nM of (circRNA-HIAT1 + its corresponding linear mRNA) with flexible circRNA-HIAT1 content were, respectively, analyzed by the PAD1 and PAD2. Fig. 4 A shows the fluorescent images and the color-shift distances of PAD1 with varying molar ratios of miRNA-21 and 1 BM sequence. The sample with 100% miRNA-21 is served as the positive control. The increased molar fractions of 1 BM sequence result in the suppressed emission color shift of PAD1. According to the 3σ criteria, the PAD1 is capable of distinguishing 1 BM sequence at a molar ratio of 25%. The minimum identification ratio of miRNA-21 was also examined by setting the sample with absolute 1 BM sequence as the negative control. As shown in Fig. 4B, the increased ratio of miRNA-21 can obviously promote the color change of PAD1. The target can be detected from the mixture even at a molar ratio as low as 0.02%. For fluorescent PAD2, similar circRNA-HIAT1 detection specificity is achieved from the positive control (Fig. 4C) and negative control (Fig. 4D) experiments. The results clearly demonstrate that the proposed fluorescent PADs possess high specificity, ascribing to the indispensable circular templates for RCA reactions. Further, the anti-interference capability of the PADs was examined by detections of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 in the coexistence of protein (bovine serum albumin, 5 mg/mL), amino acids (histidine, cysteine, both at 5 mM), DNA (100-mer, random sequence, 1 μM), and glucose (10 mM), respectively. It is found that the interference from other biological compositions is negligible (Fig. S12). Together the demonstrated high specificity and anti-interference capability enable the fluorescent PADs to analyze the clinical samples with high accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Accurate GC screening by simultaneous assays of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 in clinical samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further demonstrate the practicability of the fluorescent PADs in accurate GC screening by monitoring the expression levels of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1, we challenged the detections of RNAs in clinical plasma samples from GC patients, NSCLC patients, as well as healthy controls. On the basis of ultra-sensitivity, excellent specificity and anti-interference capability, total RNA solutions extracted from plasma were directly subjected to incubation with PAD1 or PAD2 in the plate wells, followed by RCA reaction and fluorescent labeling with gQD-sDNA1 or rQD-sDNA2, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 A, in comparison with healthy control, both samples from GC and NSCLC patients can cause the color change of PAD1 more significantly, implying the over-expression of miRNA-21 in cancer patients. However, the color shift distances of PAD1 exposed to samples from GC and NSCLC patients are rather close. The average abundance of miRNA-21 is quantified as 0.32, 0.74, and 0.70 amol/μL for healthy persons, GC, and NSCLC patients, respectively, according to the correlation between miRNA-21 concentrations and the color-shift distance. This result can be validated by the standard qRT-PCR assay (Fig. S13), which exhibits good agreement with those reported in literatures (Li et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2012). Noticeably, simplex miRNA detection is able to discover the cancerous cases but insufficient to distinguish GC from other types of cancer, suffering from the one-to-many relationship of miRNA and cancers. Fortunately, this problem is well addressed by harnessing the responses of PAD2 to circRNA-HIAT1 in the plasma. Among three groups, samples from GC patients can cause the least visible color change of PAD2 (Fig. 5B), indicating the significantly suppressed expression of circRNA-HIAT1. The level is decreased by 39% and 35% compared to those of healthy people and NSCLC patients, respectively. Clinically, the diagnostic value for miRNA-21 and the combinational diagnostic value for miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 were compared via the receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC) calculations (Fig. S14). The area under ROC curve (AUC, can reflect the true-positive rate in a diagnosis model) and Youden's index (a parameter equals to true-positive rate minus false-positive rate) for the former are 0.78 and 0.60, respectively, showing moderate diagnostic accuracy. In fact, the values could be lower, presumably the sample size becomes larger due to the pervasive up-regulation of miRNA-21 in cancers. In contrast, the AUC and Youden's index reach as high as 0.98 and 0.90, respectively, in GC diagnosis by miRNA-21 combined with circRNA-HIAT1. Therefore, the simultaneous detections of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 using the proposed fluorescent PADs provides an appropriate approach for GC screening with remarkably enhanced accuracy.

In summary, the robust fluorescent PADs, constructed by in-situ synthesis of CDs and conjugations of probe DNAs, are employed for detecting dual-type RNA molecules and highly accurate GC screening. The in-situ fluorescent functionalization of cellulose paper explored in this work provides superior uniformity, as well as boosted stability of the PADs, compared to those constructed via post-immobilization, thus further improving the reliability of RNA detections. By taking advantages of RCA and specific fluorescent labeling, the PADs were successfully applied for ultra-sensitive and ratiometric fluorescent detections of miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1, which are closely correlated to the occurrence of GC. The ratiometric fluorescence induces the distinct and continuous color changes of PADs in the presence of targets. Facilely by the color-analysis, the PADs are capable of quantifying miRNA-21 and circRNA-HIAT1 in plasma samples and further outputting the basis for accurate GC diagnose. Together with cost-effectiveness and easy-to-use, the capability of such dual-type RNA detections effectively facilitates the translation of the fluorescent PADs to point-of-care molecular diagnostics.

Yuqian Liu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Ruyi Li: Investigation, Data curation, Graph making. Fangyuan Liang: Investigation, Graph making. Chao Deng: Methodology, Investigation. Farzad Seidi: Methodology, Investigation, Validation. Huining Xiao: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have influenced the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20210630); the Metasequoia Faculty Start-up Research Fund of Nanjing Forestry University; and NSERC Canada. We thank Dr. Qu for his kind provision of clinical plasma samples.
